name: Databricks coding-agent benchmark
task_surface: Recent human-written pull requests from a multi-million-line internal codebase
data_access: Methods and aggregate claims only
conflict_note: Databricks evaluated third-party harnesses for its own engineering use and ships none of the compared systems.
weight_rationale: A buyer study on real pull requests from a multi-million-line production codebase, with matched models and broad scope.
citation_note: Directional anchor. The source publishes aggregate claims only, so cite direction and rough magnitude rather than exact points.
overview_summary: Across 6 matched comparisons, Claude Code or Codex cost 1.20 to 2.08 times as much as Pi. Quality stayed within 7 points.
slide_lead: Databricks ran 6 matched harness comparisons: Claude Code or Codex cost 1.20 to 2.08 times as much as Pi, with quality within 7 points.
method_summary: Databricks replayed recent human-written pull requests from its multi-million-line internal codebase. It compared Claude Code with Pi on Opus 4.8 and Codex with Pi on GPT-5.5 at matched effort settings.
conclusion: Pi was cheaper in all 6 published pairs. It also re-fed less context: 236,000 versus 742,000 tokens on Opus 4.8 and 665,000 versus 1.235 million on GPT-5.5.
limitation: The source does not publish task count, exact dollar cost for each point, run-level data or uncertainty.

name: Warden benchmark
date_basis: Publication date of the matched Pi and Claude SDK comparisons used here
task_surface: Known-vulnerability recognition in files tied to historical Sentry vulnerabilities
data_access: Public result table
weight_rationale: Real historical vulnerabilities in Sentry code, but the grader measures recognition of known vulnerabilities in one domain, without repeated trials.
overview_summary: Pi found a similar or larger share of known vulnerabilities at about one-quarter to one-fifth of Claude SDK's cost.
slide_lead: Sentry tested 86 known vulnerabilities: Pi found a similar or larger share while costing about one-quarter to one-fifth as much as Claude SDK.
method_summary: Sentry gave agents files connected to 86 historical vulnerabilities and measured how many known findings they recovered. The published table compares matched Claude models running in Pi and Claude SDK.
conclusion: Pi cost less and found a similar or larger share of the known corpus for the matched Claude models.
limitation: The comparator is Claude SDK, not the Claude Code command-line product. The benchmark does not measure whole-repository discovery.

name: scBench-Long
task_surface: Long biological analysis workflows
data_access: Public JSON summary and trajectories
conflict_note: LatchBio sells a bioinformatics platform in the benchmark's domain but ships none of the compared harnesses.
weight_rationale: Rigorous and transparent, with repeated trials and public trajectories, but 21 tasks in one focused domain and wide confidence intervals.
overview_summary: Claude Code led Pi on Opus 4.8, while Codex and Pi tied on GPT-5.5. The uncertainty was wide.
slide_lead: scBench-Long repeated 21 biological workflows: the apparent harness differences are smaller than the reported uncertainty.
method_summary: LatchBio ran 21 long biological analysis workflows 3 times for each model and harness combination. Deterministic biological graders checked whether the analyses reached the required outputs.
conclusion: Claude Code led Pi on Opus 4.8 while Codex and Pi tied on GPT-5.5. The reported intervals overlap widely.
limitation: The public summary does not expose cost. The confidence intervals are wide.

name: OpenBench M4.5
date_basis: Public release date of the M4.5 results used here
task_surface: Three repository coding tasks with executable checkers
data_access: Code and committed result data
weight_rationale: Three tasks that every harness passed cannot separate frontier harnesses on correctness.
overview_summary: All 4 harnesses passed every task. Pi and Cursor used less time and fewer tokens.
slide_lead: OpenBench repeated 3 coding tasks: every harness passed, so the useful difference was efficiency rather than correctness.
method_summary: OpenBench ran 3 repository coding tasks 3 times with GPT-5.5 at medium effort. Executable checkers awarded partial credit, while the study also recorded wall time and token use.
conclusion: All clean harnesses passed every task. Pi and Cursor used less time and fewer tokens.
limitation: The tasks were too easy to separate frontier harnesses on correctness.

name: DrugDiscoveryBench
task_surface: Expert-curated drug-discovery and life-sciences workflows
grader: Expert-authored rubrics scored by an LLM judge
data_access: Tasks are public; rubrics and reference answers require access approval
conflict_note: Scale AI sells evaluation and data services but ships none of the compared harnesses.
weight_rationale: Expert-curated tasks with repeated trials, but gated rubrics and an outcome that depends on an LLM judge.
overview_summary: mini-SWE-agent scored highest with GPT-5.5. Codex placed in the middle.
slide_lead: DrugDiscoveryBench repeated 82 expert workflows: mini-SWE-agent led the matched GPT-5.5 comparison, while Codex placed in the middle.
method_summary: Scale AI ran 82 expert-curated drug-discovery and life-sciences workflows 3 times. An LLM judge scored each result against expert-authored rubrics and reference answers.
conclusion: GPT-5.5 performed best in mini-SWE-agent. Codex was neither the best nor worst harness for that model.
limitation: The paper plots cost but does not publish a table of exact cost points. The outcome depends on an LLM judge.

name: Coding Agent Index
task_surface: 321 tasks across DeepSWE, Terminal-Bench v2 and SWE-Atlas-QnA
grader: Program checks, test suites and rubric scoring
data_access: Public methods and live results
conflict_note: All 3 component suites are externally produced and Artificial Analysis ships none of the compared harnesses (checked 18 July 2026).
weight_rationale: An independent evaluator running 321 tasks from 3 externally produced suites with matched models and repeated runs.
citation_note: Directional anchor. No uncertainty or task-level results are published and the live suite changes, so cite direction and rough magnitude rather than exact points. Terminal-Bench v2 results overlap the Terminal-Bench study.

component_suites.name: DeepSWE
component_suites.provenance: Human-written tasks authored from scratch on real open-source repositories

component_suites.name: Terminal-Bench v2
component_suites.provenance: Crowdsourced human-written command-line tasks; 84 of the original 89 tasks are used

component_suites.name: SWE-Atlas-QnA
component_suites.provenance: Expert-written question-answering tasks over real production repositories

overview_summary: OpenCode led with Opus 4.7; Codex led Cursor with GPT-5.5. In both comparisons, the higher-scoring harness cost more.
slide_lead: Artificial Analysis ran 321 tasks 3 times: the higher-scoring harness depended on the model and cost more in both full-suite comparisons.
method_summary: Artificial Analysis combines 321 tasks from DeepSWE, Terminal-Bench v2 and SWE-Atlas-QnA. It repeats each configuration 3 times and reports an equally weighted index, API cost and active wall time.
conclusion: OpenCode led the Opus 4.7 group by 7.7 points; Codex led Cursor with GPT-5.5 by 8.7 points. Most of both gaps came from DeepSWE.
limitation: No uncertainty or task-level results are published. The live suite, pricing and harness versions change. The GPT-5.5 match relies on the published model and effort labels.

name: Agent Security League
date_basis: Publication date of the latest matched-harness comparison used here
task_surface: Real vulnerability-fixing tasks with hidden functional and security tests
data_access: Public articles and leaderboard
conflict_note: Endor Labs ships AURI, a security layer for coding agents, not a compared harness. The benchmark page markets AURI and Endor Labs has a disclosed partnership with Cursor, a compared vendor (checked 18 July 2026).
weight_rationale: 200 real vulnerability-fixing tasks with hidden functional and security tests, but one run per configuration and a publisher with a commercial stake in the findings.
overview_summary: Cursor led on Opus 4.7 and 4.8. Claude Code led on Sonnet 5.
slide_lead: Endor Labs tested 200 vulnerability repairs: Cursor led for Opus, but Claude Code led for Sonnet 5.
method_summary: Endor Labs asked each configuration to repair 200 real vulnerabilities. Hidden tests separately measured whether the software still worked and whether the vulnerability was fixed.
conclusion: Cursor led Claude Code for Opus 4.7 and 4.8. Claude Code led Cursor for Sonnet 5.
limitation: There are no repeated trials or task-level costs. Timeouts explain part of the reversal.

name: Vulnerability-Agent-Bench
date_basis: Benchmark page publication date in the publisher sitemap
task_surface: Real vulnerability repairs in isolated containers
data_access: Public explorer and linked traces
conflict_note: XOR is a security company publishing in its own market but ships none of the compared harnesses.
weight_rationale: Real vulnerability repairs with public traces, but single runs and an inconsistent published sample count.
overview_summary: Claude Code and Codex beat OpenCode on all 3 published matched-model pairs.
slide_lead: XOR tested 136 vulnerability repairs: Claude Code and Codex beat OpenCode on all 3 published matched-model pairs.
method_summary: XOR ran agents in isolated containers on real vulnerability-repair tasks. Its public explorer reports pass rate and cost for matched model and harness configurations.
conclusion: Claude Code and Codex led the corresponding OpenCode configurations on the published matched pairs.
limitation: The site alternates between 128 and 136 samples. Some reported cost differences need more explanation.

name: WildClawBench
task_surface: Bilingual, multimodal and long-horizon agent tasks
grader: Deterministic checks, environment checks and model judges
data_access: Code, tasks, images and evaluation details
conflict_note: Hermes Agent is a Nous Research project and OpenClaw is a community project. The matched-model rows use GPT-5.4, GLM 5, MiMo V2 Pro and MiniMax M2.7, none of which is an InternLM model (checked 18 July 2026).
weight_rationale: Matched models across 4 harnesses on a broad task mix, but each harness image has different tools and prompt conventions and repeat counts are unclear.
overview_summary: The best harness changed by model. Switching harness moved scores by double digits.
slide_lead: WildClawBench tested 60 long-horizon tasks: changing the harness produced double-digit score shifts and changed the winner by model.
method_summary: InternLM evaluated 60 bilingual, multimodal and long-horizon tasks in harness-specific container images. Deterministic checks, environment checks and model judges contributed to the overall score.
conclusion: The best harness changed with the model. The source reports double-digit score changes from switching harness.
limitation: Each harness image has different tools and prompt conventions. Repeat counts are not clear in the headline table.

name: HarnessBench
task_surface: Recent bug-fix issues from 9 open-source repositories
data_access: Code and public results
weight_rationale: Recent real bug-fix issues with public code and results. The author reports the 27-task pass-rate differences as not statistically significant, so the study supports uncertainty claims rather than rankings.
overview_summary: Several configurations finished within one task of the leader. Runtime differed more clearly.
slide_lead: HarnessBench tested 27 recent bug fixes: several configurations finished within one task of the leader, but runtime differed more clearly.
method_summary: HarnessBench selected 27 recent bug-fix issues from 9 open-source repositories. Hidden tests checked the core fix and regressions, with one run for each configuration.
conclusion: Several conditions finished within one task of the observed leader. Runtime differences were clearer than pass-rate differences.
limitation: The author states that the 27-task pass-rate differences are not statistically significant.

name: CORE-Bench Hard
date_basis: Publication date of the study covering the updated scaffold comparison
task_surface: Scientific reproducibility tasks
data_access: Leaderboard and encrypted traces
weight_rationale: An independent leaderboard on 45 reproducibility tasks, but one run per configuration and compared systems that differ beyond the harness.
overview_summary: Claude Code more than doubled CORE-Agent's verified success with Opus 4.5 while costing less.
slide_lead: CORE-Bench Hard tested 45 scientific reproductions: Claude Code more than doubled verified success while costing less than CORE-Agent.
method_summary: Princeton HAL asked agents to reproduce 45 research artefacts. The leaderboard verifies whether each system recreated the required scientific result and reports total cost.
conclusion: Claude Code more than doubled the verified success of CORE-Agent for Claude Opus 4.5 while costing less.
limitation: There is one run per configuration. The systems differ across retrieval, tools and context policy.

name: A-CODE-CLI
task_surface: Ten full-stack web development scenarios
grader: Backend checks and Playwright user-interface checks
data_access: Public report and one example task
weight_rationale: Ten scenarios with only one public example task; too small to weight beyond context.
overview_summary: OpenCode had a small backend lead over Claude Code and cost 44% less with Sonnet 4.6.
slide_lead: A-CODE-CLI repeated 10 web scenarios: OpenCode had a 2.4-point backend lead and cost 44% less than Claude Code.
method_summary: AIMultiple ran 10 full-stack web development scenarios 3 times with Claude Sonnet 4.6. Backend checks and Playwright tests measured correctness and user-interface behaviour.
conclusion: OpenCode had a small backend lead over Claude Code and cost less in the matched Sonnet 4.6 comparison.
limitation: The benchmark has only 10 scenarios. Only one example task is public.

name: Harness Tax
task_surface: Two trivial prompts routed through a gateway
data_access: Method and aggregate values
conflict_note: Portkey sells the gateway used to capture the measurements but ships none of the compared harnesses.
weight_rationale: A 2-prompt overhead diagnostic that measures fixed input tokens, not coding quality.
overview_summary: Fixed input overhead ranged from about 2,600 tokens for Pi to 27,000 for Claude Code.
slide_lead: Portkey measured 2 trivial requests: fixed input overhead ranged from about 2,600 tokens for Pi to 27,000 for Claude Code.
method_summary: Portkey routed 2 trivial prompts through its gateway and recorded the input payload sent by each harness. The exercise isolates fixed prompt overhead rather than coding performance.
conclusion: The fixed input payload ranged from about 2,600 tokens for Pi to 27,000 for Claude Code.
limitation: This is an overhead diagnostic. It does not measure coding quality or full-session economics.

name: GitHub Copilot harness evaluation
task_surface: Five coding and terminal benchmark suites, including SWE-bench and Terminal-Bench 2
grader: Public benchmark graders and internal executable checks
data_access: Methods and exact relative-result charts; no raw rows
conflict_note: GitHub ships Copilot CLI, one of the compared harnesses.
weight_rationale: A broad matched study with repeated runs, but published by a vendor of a compared harness and without raw result rows.
citation_note: Cite only suites with at least 100 tasks. For smaller suites the source reports the best of 5 runs while labelling the metric pass@1, so treat those figures as contextual.
overview_summary: Across 20 matched pairs, Copilot CLI moved task resolution by -8.0 to +7.1 points and used fewer median tokens in 18.
slide_lead: GitHub compared Copilot CLI with Claude Code or Codex across 5 suites: quality was mixed, while Copilot used fewer median tokens in 18 of 20 pairs.
method_summary: GitHub fixed the model, task, context limit, reasoning effort and environment across Copilot CLI and each model provider's native harness. It tested 4 models on 5 suites and ran each configuration 5 times.
conclusion: Task-resolution differences ranged from 8.0 points worse to 7.1 points better. Copilot used fewer median tokens in 18 of 20 model and benchmark pairs.
limitation: This is a vendor study with no raw result rows. For suites under 100 tasks, GitHub reports the best of 5 runs while labelling the metric pass@1.

name: Terminal-Bench 2.1
task_surface: Containerized command-line tasks with deterministic final-state checks
data_access: Public leaderboard, paper, experiments and submissions
weight_rationale: An independent benchmark with a public paper, leaderboard, experiments and submissions, deterministic grading and repeated trials.
overview_summary: The native CLI led 4 of 6 current matched pairs; 1 was effectively tied and Terminus 2 led Gemini 3 Pro.
slide_lead: Terminal-Bench repeated 89 command-line tasks: the native CLI led 4 of 6 current matched pairs, but not for every model.
method_summary: Terminal-Bench runs 89 containerized terminal tasks with deterministic final-state tests. The current 2.1 leaderboard contains 6 clean same-model pairs between a provider CLI and Terminus 2, with at least 5 runs per configuration.
conclusion: Claude Code or Codex led 4 pairs. Gemini 3.1 Pro was effectively tied, while Terminus 2 led Gemini 3 Pro by 8.1 points.
limitation: The leaderboard changes over time. A token appendix in the 2.0 paper says it covers 74 tasks, not the full 89, so this review does not derive per-task token values from it.

name: Agents' Last Exam
task_surface: Long-horizon professional workflows across CLI, GUI, files, applications and web research
data_access: Public paper, tables, code and data
weight_rationale: An academic study of 152 long-horizon tasks across several domains, with matched models and public paper, code and data.
overview_summary: GPT-5.5 full-pass rates spanned 19.1% to 24.0%, while published total cost spanned $180 to $570.
slide_lead: Agents' Last Exam tested long professional workflows: GPT-5.5 harnesses stayed within 4.9 points, but total cost differed by more than 3 times.
method_summary: Agents' Last Exam evaluates long professional workflows with runs capped at 5 hours. It reports fixed-model harness sweeps for GPT-5.5 and Opus 4.7, plus a 105-task ALE-CLI sweep for Sonnet 4.6.
conclusion: The harness changed both full-pass rate and resources, but the model effect could be larger: GPT-5.5 harnesses were within 4.9 points while the fixed-OpenClaw model spread was 16.8 points.
limitation: Only some configurations have 3 independent runs. The published tier counts do not add cleanly to the stated 152-task public set.

name: Claw-SWE-Bench
task_surface: Multilingual GitHub issue-resolution tasks
data_access: Public paper, adapters, dataset and result artefacts
weight_rationale: 350 real multilingual issue-resolution tasks with public artefacts, but one attempt per task and no confidence intervals.
citation_note: Treat token and cost values as contextual: the source's token accounting contains large harness-specific anomalies.
overview_summary: The harness spread was 12.5 points with GLM-5.1 and 27.4 points with Qwen3.6-Flash.
slide_lead: Claw-SWE-Bench crossed 5 harnesses with 2 models on 350 issues: the harness spread grew from 12.5 to 27.4 points depending on the model.
method_summary: Claw-SWE-Bench fully crossed 5 harnesses and 2 models on the same 350 multilingual GitHub issues, with a fixed prompt, workspace contract, runtime budget, patch extraction and evaluator.
conclusion: OpenClaw led both model groups. The gap between best and worst harness was 12.5 points for GLM-5.1 and 27.4 points for Qwen3.6-Flash.
limitation: There is one attempt per task and configuration, with no confidence intervals. Token accounting contains large harness-specific anomalies.

name: Harness-Bench paper
task_surface: Executable office, retrieval, analytics, SRE and software-engineering workflows
data_access: Public paper, tasks, code and aggregated JSON
conflict_note: The 6 harnesses and 8 models are attributed to parties other than Qihoo 360 (checked 18 July 2026). The authors' Qiyuan Tech affiliation could not be conclusively distinguished from Beijing Qiyuan Technology, a 360-linked entity.
weight_rationale: 106 executable tasks across 6 harnesses and 8 models, but one trajectory per configuration and a combined score that includes process judging.
overview_summary: Across the same 8-model pool, average combined score ranged from 52.4 for OpenClaw to 76.2 for NanoBot.
slide_lead: Harness-Bench ran a 6-harness by 8-model factorial: average combined score ranged from 52.4 to 76.2 across harnesses.
method_summary: Harness-Bench ran 6 configurable harnesses with 8 models on 106 executable professional tasks, producing 5,088 trajectories. Only 22 tasks are software engineering.
conclusion: NanoBot had the highest harness-average combined score and completion rate. The size and direction of the harness effect still changed by model.
limitation: There is one trajectory per task and configuration. Combined score includes process judging, and the public repository currently has no licence file.

name: Harvey LAB harness ablation
task_surface: Held-out legal workflows scored against expert-written criteria
grader: GPT-5 Mini against expert criteria, graded 3 times and averaged
data_access: Public aggregate chart; benchmark tasks and baseline harness are public
conflict_note: Applied Compute, a co-publisher, built the improved harness under test.
weight_rationale: A vendor comparison with an aggregate-only chart, undefined error values and a non-public test split and harness.
overview_summary: The same harness intervention ranged from 0.9 points worse to 23 points better, depending on the model.
slide_lead: Harvey tested 180 legal tasks: the Applied Compute harness ranged from 0.9 points worse to 23 points better across 6 fixed models.
method_summary: Harvey compared its baseline LAB-style harness with an Applied Compute harness on 180 held-out legal tasks and about 10,000 criteria. It held 6 models and the grader fixed.
conclusion: The treatment effect was heterogeneous: slightly negative for GLM 5.1, neutral for Kimi K2.6 and strongly positive for Sonnet 4.6.
limitation: The exact test split and Applied Compute harness are not public. Published error values are not defined, and agent cost, runtime and tokens are not reported.

name: CSI scaffold comparison
task_surface: CyBench capture-the-flag security challenges
data_access: Public paper and project page; no dedicated experiment artefact found
conflict_note: Both compared scaffolds are Alias Robotics products; the comparison is between its own backends.
weight_rationale: A vendor comparison of 33 challenges with one run per scaffold and no public experiment artefact.
overview_summary: CSI::Claude and CSI::Codex both solved 15 of 33 tasks, but Codex cost one-third as much and finished faster.
slide_lead: CSI ran 5 scaffolds with one model on 33 security challenges: Claude and Codex tied on solves, while Codex cost one-third as much.
method_summary: Alias Robotics ran 5 scaffolds with the same private alias2-mini model on 33 CyBench capture-the-flag challenges and recorded solves, total API cost, wall time, commands and tokens.
conclusion: CSI::Claude and CSI::Codex both solved 15 tasks. CSI::Codex used one-third of the published cost and 31% less wall time.
limitation: There is one run per scaffold and challenge. The model is proprietary, the task set is small and no dedicated experiment artefact is public.

name: The Scaffold Effect
task_surface: A 50-task slice of Terminal-Bench Pro
data_access: Public paper, raw results, prompts and analysis
weight_rationale: A public 50-task slice with raw results, but one run per configuration and incomplete token accounting.
overview_summary: Goose used about 41 times fewer reported tokens than OpenCode for similar pass rates on 2 fixed models.
slide_lead: The Scaffold Effect ran 300 trials: Goose used about 41 times fewer reported tokens than OpenCode, with pass rates within 2 to 8 points.
method_summary: The study ran 2 models through Goose, OpenCode and OpenHands-SDK on a stratified 50-task slice of Terminal-Bench Pro, with identical environments and a 40-turn budget.
conclusion: Resource use separated the harnesses more clearly than pass rate. OpenCode used about 41 times as many reported tokens as Goose.
limitation: There is one run per task and configuration. Token accounting differs by harness and is incomplete; cost is missing for 2 harnesses.

name: HAL SWE-bench Verified Mini
task_surface: A fixed 50-task subset of SWE-bench Verified
data_access: Public leaderboard and downloadable traces
weight_rationale: An independent leaderboard with downloadable traces, but a 50-task subset with mostly single runs.
overview_summary: SWE-Agent usually solved more tasks than HAL Generalist, but often at several times the reported cost.
slide_lead: HAL compared 15 models on 50 SWE-bench tasks: SWE-Agent usually solved more, while HAL Generalist usually cost less.
method_summary: Princeton HAL ran the same 50 SWE-bench Verified Mini tasks under SWE-Agent and HAL Generalist for 15 models and effort settings, reporting pass rate and total provider cost.
conclusion: SWE-Agent led 14 of 15 matched pairs on pass rate. HAL Generalist was often cheaper, showing a strong quality-cost trade-off rather than a universal harness win.
limitation: Most entries have one evaluation run. SWE-Agent cost excludes caching, and the archived trace repository has no licence file.
